# Murdoku — full reference for generating puzzle cases as JSON

This document gives a language model everything it needs to generate a valid
Murdoku case from a natural-language prompt. It explains (1) what Murdoku is and
its rules, (2) the exact JSON schema the Murdoku Studio app imports, (3) the
constraints a valid case must satisfy, and (4) guidance for writing good clues.
A complete worked example is at the end.

================================================================================
1. WHAT IS MURDOKU
================================================================================

Murdoku is a logic puzzle: "a murder mystery that plays like Sudoku." The solver
places a cast of named people onto a grid using clue cards, then identifies the
murderer.

Core rules:

- THE GRID. An R×C grid is the crime scene (commonly 6×6; 3–9 each side). Cells
  are addressed by (row, column). Row 1 is the TOP row; column 1 is the LEFT
  column.

- PEOPLE. There are several suspects plus exactly ONE victim. Every person —
  suspects AND the victim — occupies one cell.

- ROW/COLUMN CONSTRAINT (the "Sudoku" rule). At most one person per row and at
  most one person per column. So the set of people forms a partial permutation:
  no two people share a row, and no two share a column. In a "full" case the
  number of people equals the grid side (e.g. 6 people on a 6×6 grid), so every
  row and every column holds exactly one person.

- REGIONS. The grid is divided into named, colored areas called regions (e.g.
  "Living Room", "Kitchen", "Bedroom"). Every relevant cell belongs to a region.
  Regions group cells and are referenced by clues.

- OBJECTS. Objects (shelf, bed, chair, TV, table, …) sit on cells and are
  referenced by clues. An object is either "occupiable" (a person can stand on
  its cell — bed, chair, sofa, table) or scenery that cannot be occupied (shelf,
  TV, plant, …). A person may share a cell with an occupiable object.

- THE MURDER CONDITION. The murderer is the suspect who is ALONE WITH THE VICTIM:
  the victim's region contains exactly ONE suspect, and that suspect is the
  murderer. (The victim's own card always reads "…was alone with the murderer.")
  Therefore a valid case MUST place the victim inside a region that contains
  exactly one suspect.

- CLUES. Each suspect carries one free-text clue card describing where they were.
  The victim's clue is generated automatically and must not be supplied. There
  may also be "general clues" — scene-wide facts not tied to one suspect. Clues
  are prose; the solver reads them to deduce placements.

================================================================================
2. THE JSON SCHEMA (authoring format)
================================================================================

Produce a single JSON object with this shape. This is the "authoring format"
that the Studio's Import accepts directly.

{
  "title": string,                      // case name, e.g. "Netflix and Kill"
  "grid": { "rows": int, "cols": int }, // 3..9 each

  "regions": [
    {
      "name": string,                   // e.g. "Living Room"
      "color": string,                  // hex color, e.g. "#d6f0ea" (optional; auto if omitted)
      "cells": [[row, col], ...]        // 1-based [row, col] pairs that belong to this region
    }
  ],

  "objects": [
    {
      "type": string,                   // lowercase noun, e.g. "shelf", "bed", "chair"
      "emoji": string,                  // single emoji shown on the cell, e.g. "📚" (optional; default 🏠)
      "occupiable": boolean,            // true = a person may stand here; false = scenery (optional; default true)
      "row": int, "col": int            // 1-based cell the object sits on
    }
  ],

  "people": [
    {
      "name": string,                   // e.g. "Austin"
      "color": string,                  // hex token color (optional; auto if omitted)
      "row": int, "col": int,           // 1-based cell the person occupies
      "victim": boolean,                // true for the single victim; omit/false for suspects
      "clue": string                    // the suspect's clue card (omit for the victim)
    }
  ],

  "generalClues": [ string, ... ]       // optional scene-wide clues
}

NOTES ON COORDINATES
- ALL coordinates in this format are 1-BASED: row 1 = top, col 1 = left.
- A region's "cells" are [row, col] pairs. List every cell that belongs to it.
- Regions should not overlap; ideally every cell belongs to exactly one region.

NOTES ON DIRECTIONS (use consistently in clue prose)
- "north" = up = a smaller row number;  "south" = down = a larger row number.
- "west"  = left = a smaller column;     "east"  = right = a larger column.
- "beside" / "next to" = orthogonally adjacent (up/down/left/right) AND in the
  same region. (Diagonal is NOT beside.)
- "the last column" = column = cols; "the top row" = row 1; "the bottom row" =
  row = rows.

================================================================================
3. VALIDITY CONSTRAINTS (a generated case MUST satisfy all of these)
================================================================================

1. grid.rows and grid.cols are between 3 and 9.
2. There is exactly ONE person with "victim": true.
3. There are one or more suspects (people without "victim": true).
4. Every person has a row and col within the grid.
5. ROW/COLUMN RULE: no two people share a row; no two people share a column
   (the victim counts). Tip: choose people positions as a permutation.
6. The victim's cell lies inside some region, and EXACTLY ONE suspect lies in
   that same region. That suspect is the murderer. (No suspect in the victim's
   region = unsolvable; two or more = ambiguous. Both are invalid.)
7. Every suspect has a non-empty "clue". The victim has NO clue (auto-generated).
8. Objects referenced by clues should actually be placed on the board, and clue
   prose should be TRUE for the stated placement (e.g. if a clue says "beside the
   shelf", that person's cell must be orthogonally adjacent to a shelf cell in
   the same region).
9. The full clue set should ideally determine a unique arrangement, but the app
   does not verify uniqueness for free-text clues — that is the author's craft.
   Aim for clues that, taken together, pin every suspect to one cell.

================================================================================
4. WRITING GOOD CLUES
================================================================================

Each suspect gets ONE clue, written in natural past tense with a pronoun or the
phrasing of your choice. Mix these clue flavors so the puzzle is solvable by
elimination:

- On an object:        "She was on the bed."        (person's cell holds that object)
- Beside an object:    "He was beside a shelf."      (adjacent + same region)
- Only one at object:  "She was the only person sitting in a chair."
- In a region:         "He was in the Kitchen."
- Column / row:        "She was in the last column."  /  "He was in row 2."
- Relative to a person:"He was directly north of Mara."  /  "She sat two columns east of Dean."
- Relative direction:  "He was somewhere south of the victim."

The victim's card is always, automatically: "They were alone with the murderer."
(or "He/She was…" if you set the victim's gender in the Studio — but you do not
supply the victim clue in JSON).

General clues are optional scene-wide statements, e.g.:
- "The cash register was untouched — robbery wasn't the motive."
- "Nobody stood in the doorway."

GENERATION RECIPE
1. Pick a theme, a grid size N×N, and N people (N-1 suspects + 1 victim).
2. Lay out regions covering the grid; give the murder its own region containing
   the victim and exactly one suspect.
3. Choose people positions as a permutation (one per row, one per column).
4. Place a few objects to give clues something to reference; mark which are
   occupiable.
5. Write one TRUE, distinguishing clue per suspect. Add 0–3 general clues.
6. Double-check every constraint in section 3.

================================================================================
5. COMPLETE EXAMPLE — "Netflix and Kill" (6×6, very easy)
================================================================================

Murderer (by the rules, not stored as a clue): Barbara — she is the only suspect
in the Bedroom, where the victim Vaughn lies.

{
  "title": "Netflix and Kill",
  "grid": { "rows": 6, "cols": 6 },
  "regions": [
    {
      "name": "Living Room",
      "color": "#d6f0ea",
      "cells": [[1,1],[1,2],[1,3],[1,4],[2,1],[2,2],[2,3],[2,4],
                [3,1],[3,2],[3,3],[3,4],[4,1],[4,2],[4,3],[4,4]]
    },
    {
      "name": "Kitchen",
      "color": "#d8edcb",
      "cells": [[1,5],[1,6],[2,5],[2,6],[3,5],[3,6],
                [4,5],[4,6],[5,5],[5,6],[6,5],[6,6]]
    },
    {
      "name": "Bedroom",
      "color": "#e6dcf0",
      "cells": [[5,1],[5,2],[5,3],[5,4],[6,1],[6,2],[6,3],[6,4]]
    }
  ],
  "objects": [
    { "type": "tv",    "emoji": "📺", "occupiable": false, "row": 1, "col": 1 },
    { "type": "sofa",  "emoji": "🛋️", "occupiable": true,  "row": 2, "col": 2 },
    { "type": "shelf", "emoji": "📚", "occupiable": false, "row": 3, "col": 3 },
    { "type": "chair", "emoji": "🪑", "occupiable": true,  "row": 4, "col": 2 },
    { "type": "bed",   "emoji": "🛏️", "occupiable": true,  "row": 5, "col": 1 },
    { "type": "table", "emoji": "🍽️", "occupiable": true,  "row": 2, "col": 5 }
  ],
  "people": [
    { "name": "Austin",    "row": 3, "col": 4, "clue": "He was beside the shelf." },
    { "name": "Barbara",   "row": 5, "col": 1, "clue": "She was on the bed." },
    { "name": "Charlotte", "row": 4, "col": 2, "clue": "She was the only person sitting in a chair." },
    { "name": "Dean",      "row": 2, "col": 5, "clue": "He was in the Kitchen." },
    { "name": "Enid",      "row": 1, "col": 6, "clue": "She was in the last column." },
    { "name": "Vaughn",    "row": 6, "col": 3, "victim": true }
  ],
  "generalClues": [
    "The TV was still playing when the body was found.",
    "The cash register was untouched — robbery wasn't the motive."
  ]
}

Why this is valid:
- 6 people on a 6×6 grid; rows used {3,5,4,2,1,6} all distinct; columns used
  {4,1,2,5,6,3} all distinct → row/column rule holds.
- Victim Vaughn is at (6,3) in the Bedroom. Suspects in the Bedroom: only Barbara
  (5,1). Charlotte (4,2) is in the Living Room. → exactly one suspect with the
  victim → Barbara is the murderer.
- Each suspect's clue is true: Austin (3,4) is orthogonally adjacent to the shelf
  at (3,3) in the same region; Barbara (5,1) is on the bed at (5,1); Charlotte
  (4,2) is on the only chair; Dean (2,5) is in the Kitchen; Enid (1,6) is in the
  last column (6).

To use a generated case: open Murdoku Studio → Case actions → Import → "Load from
.json" (or paste the JSON) → Import.
